title: Is artistic talent innate?
subtitle: For questions 1–8, read the text and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).
passage:
It is often said that pretty much anyone can learn to draw or paint [[0]], given enough training and practice. However, there are some for whom such skills [[1]] naturally from an early age. These children, known to researchers as precocious realists, have been the focus of much study to [[2]] what they do differently from their peers.

One striking difference is their focus on the tiniest of details in their art, even at the expense of the wider context. A typical child will sketch a complete object, such as an apple, and feel a sense of [[3]] once it is broadly recognisable. For a precocious realist, however, it is the [[4]] of light and shadow, for example, or the [[5]] on the apple's skin that are most [[6]].

Another key difference is the [[7]] drive for perfection. Precocious realists are obsessed with the mastery of their skills – not just to capture a realistic likeness of an object, but to express their own emotions in the process. Thus they are motivated to spend every waking hour [[8]] their skills.

items.explainCorrect: Come naturally is the set phrase: appear or develop without being forced.

explainIfChosen.0: Skills do not normally rise naturally in this fixed expression.
explainIfChosen.1: Hold naturally does not collocate with acquiring a skill.
explainIfChosen.3: Fall naturally suggests the wrong direction of meaning here.

items.explainCorrect: Ascertain = find out for certain; research aims to discover what differs in their behaviour.

explainIfChosen.0: Substantiate means provide proof for something already claimed — weaker fit than discovering facts.
explainIfChosen.2: Affirm stresses declaring something true, not investigating differences.
explainIfChosen.3: Justify means defend as right — not the goal of observational study here.

items.explainCorrect: Sense of gratification = satisfaction when the drawing is good enough.

explainIfChosen.1: Indulgence usually implies treating yourself, not finishing a simple sketch.
explainIfChosen.2: Fruition is about a plan reaching completion — odd with “once it is recognisable”.
explainIfChosen.3: Acquisition is gaining a thing, not an inner feeling of reward.

items.explainCorrect: Juxtaposition = placing things side by side so contrast shows — ideal for light and shadow.

explainIfChosen.0: Proximity is mere nearness; it does not highlight contrast the way the sentence does.
explainIfChosen.1: Collaboration involves people working together — irrelevant to light on an apple.
explainIfChosen.2: Reciprocation is give-and-take between parties — wrong domain.

items.explainCorrect: Blemishes = small marks or imperfections artists study closely on a surface.

explainIfChosen.0: Speckles are possible, but blemishes better matches minute realistic detail in formal prose.
explainIfChosen.2: Eyesores are ugly whole objects, not tiny skin detail.
explainIfChosen.3: Spatters suggests messy drops, not subtle natural texture.

items.explainCorrect: Salient = most noticeable or important — what draws the precocious realist’s eye.

explainIfChosen.0: Flagrant means blatantly unacceptable — wrong for neutral visual detail.
explainIfChosen.1: Notorious means famously bad — not a fit for artistic focus.
explainIfChosen.3: Intrusive suggests unwelcome interruption, not “most prominent” in the picture.

items.explainCorrect: Boundless drive = limitless motivation — matches obsession with perfection.

explainIfChosen.0: Interminable often implies tedious endlessness, not energetic passion.
explainIfChosen.2: Incessant can sound nagging or repetitive rather than deep inner drive.
explainIfChosen.3: Protracted stresses length in time, not the scale of ambition.

items.explainCorrect: Hone skills = sharpen and refine them through practice — standard collocation.

explainIfChosen.1: Kindling starts a fire or spark — not the usual verb with skills.
explainIfChosen.2: Piquing usually takes interest or curiosity as object.
explainIfChosen.3: Whetting pairs with appetite or desire, not skills.
